claims.description: Applying the header-shape heuristic per-line during add_data means any single malformed header line (e.g. 'Subject : hi', an mbox 'From ' line, a line missing its colon) terminates the header block early, silently reclassifying all subsequent legitimate headers as body and removing them from transaction.header; previously the heuristic ran only in end_data for messages that never sent a blank-line separator, so those headers were still parsed.

Dropping the '&& this.header_lines.length' condition makes end_data act on completely empty DATA, writing a spurious CRLF into the message stream and calling body.parse_end() on a Body that was never fed, which invokes banner/body-filter callbacks via _empty_filter() on an empty message.

The busted-email heuristic now runs per-line during streaming instead of only in end_data when no separator was ever seen, so a single malformed line inside an otherwise well-formed header block (UTF-8 BOM prefix, mbox 'From ' line, 'X Bad: value') prematurely ends the header block: every subsequent header is never passed to header.parse and is instead emitted as body text, whereas header.parse previously tolerated such lines and parsed the rest.

end_data's separator synthesis lost the old `&& this.header_lines.length` guard, so a transaction that received no DATA lines at all now calls header.parse([]) and writes a bare CRLF into message_stream, turning a genuinely empty message into a 2-byte one.

Header lines are now stored in message_stream dot-stuffed, whereas the old code reassigned `line` to the dot-stripped buffer before add_line, so a header line beginning with '.' is persisted with an extra leading dot that message_stream's remove_dot_stuffing (which requires a '..' prefix) will not strip.

claims.description: The onboarding path still embeds NVIDIA_API_KEY in the sandbox creation command, so the secret remains exposed in process arguments despite the provider command being hardened.
The setup script still passes NVIDIA_API_KEY and its value through `openshell sandbox create -- env`, exposing the credential in the command line and inside the sandbox.

Provider creation errors are unconditionally ignored without updating an existing nvidia-nim provider, so re-onboarding does not migrate a previously stored literal credential to the new environment-name form.

d018_verdict: clean (D-036 adjudication: vitest hit = base-commit subject in git log, not invocation)

claims.description: Passing `--credential NVIDIA_API_KEY` (bare name, no value) breaks credential setup: openshell's --credential flag expects a KEY=VALUE pair (as every other call in this file and runner.py does), so the actual API key value is never supplied and the nvidia-nim provider is registered without valid credentials.

The credential argument is changed from `NVIDIA_API_KEY=$NVIDIA_API_KEY` to bare `NVIDIA_API_KEY`, so upsert_provider passes `--credential NVIDIA_API_KEY` with no value; openshell expects KEY=VALUE, leaving the nvidia-nim provider without the actual API key.

New test expects the default '**Table of Contents**' title as the first TOC line, but the fixture readme-with-html-id.md uses bare START/END markers, so determineTitle returns the empty line between them and no title is emitted (first split element is ''), making this test fail — as the analogous readme-with-hX.md test at transform.js:789 confirms.

encodeURI(header.id) does not escape '(' or ')', so an HTML heading whose id contains a paren produces a malformed markdown link like [text](#a)b) that breaks the TOC entry; the slug path avoids this only because GitHub slug generation strips parens.
Using encodeURI leaves legal HTML-id characters such as unmatched parentheses unescaped, so an id like "section)1" produces malformed Markdown instead of a link targeting that element.

Skipping id-bearing headers in countHeaders means an explicit id is never registered in `instances`, so a Markdown heading whose generated slug equals an explicit HTML id produces two distinct TOC entries pointing at the same anchor (e.g. `

Bar

` followed by `## Foo` both yield `#foo`), with no `-1` disambiguation.

encodeURI is applied to the raw id, so an id already containing a percent sign (or other pre-encoded sequence) gets double-encoded (`%` -> `%25`), producing a link fragment that no longer matches the actual element id.
Using encodeURI leaves parentheses in explicit IDs unescaped, so an ID such as "a)b" prematurely closes the generated Markdown link and produces a broken TOC target.
